shared_with: Government primary, shared with space operations
description: NOAA POES Automatic Picture Transmission (APT) downlink. NOAA-15 at 137.62 MHz, NOAA-18 at 137.9125 MHz, NOAA-19 at 137.1 MHz. Analog FM weather images receivable with simple equipment. Also used by Meteor-M Russian weather satellites. Popular with amateur satellite enthusiasts.
source: NOAA NESDIS satellite frequencies; allmetsat.com frequency list

shared_with: Government exclusive allocation
description: Primary military UHF tactical communications band. Used by all services for air-to-air, air-to-ground, ground-to-ground voice and data. Includes HAVE QUICK frequency hopping for anti-jam. SINCGARS-adjacent. Also includes military satellite uplinks/downlinks (240-270/290-320 overlap). Dense usage — one of the most congested military bands.
source: NTIA frequency allocation table

shared_with: Government exclusive
description: UHF Military Satellite Communications. Downlink 240-270 MHz, uplink 290-320 MHz. Operated via UFO (UHF Follow-On) and MUOS (Mobile User Objective System) constellations. Provides tactical narrowband communications to mobile forces (ground, naval, airborne). MUOS added WCDMA waveform for 3G-like mobile capability. Approximately 39 channels per satellite.
source: NTIA frequency allocation table; RadioReference UHF-MILSATCOM wiki

shared_with: Government exclusive for aeronautical radionavigation, shared with DME/TACAN
description: Aeronautical radionavigation band. Includes DME (Distance Measuring Equipment), TACAN (Tactical Air Navigation), IFF (Identification Friend or Foe) at 1030/1090 MHz, ADS-B at 1090 MHz. Also includes UAT (Universal Access Transceiver) at 978 MHz for ADS-B below 18,000 ft. Critical aviation safety band — heavily protected from commercial encroachment.
source: NTIA frequency allocation table; FAA spectrum management

shared_with: Civil aviation transponders (Mode A/C/S), ADS-B (1090 MHz)
description: Secondary Surveillance Radar (SSR) and ADS-B. Interrogator transmits at 1030 MHz, transponder replies at 1090 MHz. Mode S provides selective interrogation. ADS-B Out at 1090 MHz (extended squitter) now mandatory for most US airspace. Also used by TCAS (Traffic Collision Avoidance System).
source: FAA Order 6050.12; NTIA frequency allocation table

shared_with: Civilian safety-of-life (aviation), ARNS band
description: GPS L5 signal: civilian safety-of-life signal at 1176.45 MHz. Higher power than L1 C/A. In the Aeronautical Radio Navigation Service (ARNS) band, protected for aviation. Designed for precision approach guidance. Available on Block IIF and later GPS satellites.
source: NTIA frequency allocation table; GPS.gov

shared_with: GPS L2/L5 overlay, radionavigation, radiolocation
description: L-band military radar. Used for long-range air defense surveillance, ballistic missile early warning, space surveillance. Includes AN/FPS-132 (PAVE PAWS), BMEWS radars. Also ARSR (Air Route Surveillance Radar) for FAA/DoD joint use. 1215-1240 MHz heavily used by tactical military radars. Band is government/non-government shared for radiolocation.
source: NTIA compendium 1215-1240 MHz; NTIA Federal Radar Spectrum Requirements (NTIA 00-40)

shared_with: Civilian L2C receivers (modernized signal)
description: GPS L2 signal: P(Y) code (military encrypted), L2C (civilian modernized). Center frequency 1227.60 MHz. Used for dual-frequency ionospheric correction. Military receivers use L2 P(Y) for precision. Civilian L2C available on Block IIR-M and later satellites.
source: NTIA frequency allocation table; GPS.gov

shared_with: Civilian GPS receivers, Galileo E1 overlap
description: GPS L1 signal: C/A code (civilian), P(Y) code (military encrypted), L1C (modernized civil). Center frequency 1575.42 MHz. Primary civilian and military positioning signal. All GPS satellites transmit on L1.
source: NTIA frequency allocation table; GPS.gov

shared_with: Government primary; 1675-1680 MHz identified for potential auction by NTIA under OBBBA
description: NOAA meteorological satellite downlink band. GOES-R series HRIT/EMWIN at 1694.1 MHz. POES HRPT (High Resolution Picture Transmission) real-time data broadcast. GOES DCS (Data Collection System) downlink at 1679.7-1680.1 MHz. Critical for national weather forecasting, severe weather warnings, and climate monitoring. NTIA identified 1675-1680 MHz as first band for reallocation study.
source: NTIA compendium 1675-1700 MHz; NOAA NESDIS specifications; GOES-R GRB specifications

shared_with: NTIA identified for study under OBBBA pipeline
description: NOAA meteorological operations and radiosonde (weather balloon) frequencies. One of four bands NTIA disclosed for study under OBBBA 800 MHz spectrum pipeline alongside 2.7-2.9 GHz, 4.4-4.94 GHz, and 7.125-7.4 GHz. Active weather data collection operations would need relocation or protection framework.
source: NTIA OBBBA spectrum pipeline identification

shared_with: Reallocated to shared Federal/non-Federal for AWS-3 auction (Auction 97); co-primary with mobile broadband
description: Former NOAA-exclusive MetSat band, partially reallocated for commercial broadband in AWS-3 auction (2015). AWS-3 raised $44.9B. NOAA retains some operations with protection criteria. Example of government-to-commercial spectrum transition. 1695-1710 paired with 1755-1780 for AWS-3.
source: NTIA compendium 1695-1710 MHz; FCC Auction 97 results

shared_with: 1755-1780 MHz reallocated for AWS-3 shared use; DoD retains operations with coordination
description: Military tactical relay, satellite TT&C (telemetry tracking and command), UAV control links, precision guided munitions datalinks. 1755-1780 MHz was partially reallocated for AWS-3 auction but DoD retains access with coordination framework. Ongoing tension between commercial demand and military operational requirements. Critical for drone operations.
source: NTIA frequency allocation table; AWS-3 auction framework

shared_with: Government-only primary allocation, commercial weather radar (NEXRAD WSR-88D)
description: Airport Surveillance Radar band. FAA operates ASR-8, ASR-9, ASR-11 systems for terminal area air traffic control. Also NEXRAD WSR-88D weather radar (NWS/FAA/DoD jointly). S-band primary radar with 60-200 nmi range. Critical for aviation safety. This band is one NTIA identified for study under OBBBA spectrum pipeline (2.7-2.9 GHz).
source: NTIA compendium 2700-2900 MHz; FAA ASR-11 specifications

shared_with: CBRS sharing (3550-3700), FAA ASR, NEXRAD weather radar
description: S-band government radar allocation. Includes FAA ASR (2700-2900), naval shipborne radar (2900-3100), DoD tactical radar (3100-3300), CBRS band with spectrum sharing (3550-3700). The 3100-3450 MHz segment under study for potential commercial sharing per NTIA National Spectrum Strategy. DoD operates ship-based fire control and tracking radars throughout.
source: NTIA Federal Radar Spectrum Requirements; NTIA compendium 3100-3300 MHz

shared_with: Under study for commercial sharing; EXCLUDED from OBBBA mandatory reallocation
description: DoD S-band radar. Naval shipborne radar (AN/SPN-43 carrier-controlled approach, AN/SPQ-9B). Ground-based air defense radars. DoD determined sharing is technically feasible with advanced interference mitigation but requires robust coordination framework. NTIA studying for National Spectrum Strategy. Congress protected from mandatory reallocation in OBBBA (excluded from 800 MHz pipeline). Studies due October 2026.
source: NTIA National Spectrum Strategy; OBBBA spectrum provisions; DoD sharing feasibility report

shared_with: Three-tier sharing: Incumbent (Navy radar/FSS), Priority Access License (PAL), General Authorized Access (GAA)
description: Citizens Broadband Radio Service — the US model for dynamic spectrum sharing. Navy SPN-43 radar is incumbent with highest priority. Environmental Sensing Capability (ESC) sensors detect naval radar and trigger commercial devices to vacate. PAL licenses auctioned in Auction 105 ($4.6B, 2020). GAA is unlicensed access at lowest tier. SAS (Spectrum Access System) manages sharing in real-time. 150 MHz total bandwidth.
source: FCC 47 CFR Part 96; Auction 105 results; SAS operator data

shared_with: Government exclusive; NTIA identified for study under OBBBA pipeline
description: Military troposcatter communications, point-to-point microwave, tactical communications relay. One of four bands NTIA disclosed for study under OBBBA 800 MHz spectrum pipeline. Potential future sharing or reallocation candidate for 5G expansion. DoD use includes long-range beyond-line-of-sight communications critical for expeditionary forces.
source: NTIA OBBBA spectrum pipeline identification; NTIA frequency allocation table

shared_with: NTIA identified for study under OBBBA pipeline; adjacent to 6 GHz unlicensed upper edge
description: Government fixed and mobile communications band immediately above the 6 GHz unlicensed band (which ends at 7.125 GHz). One of four bands NTIA disclosed for study under OBBBA 800 MHz spectrum pipeline. Potential expansion of unlicensed or licensed commercial use above 7.125 GHz. Adjacent to WiFi 6E/7 upper boundary — industry interest in extending UNII operations into this range.
source: NTIA OBBBA spectrum pipeline identification; NTIA frequency allocation table

shared_with: Government primary, some shared with fixed service
description: X-band military satellite communications. Downlink 7.25-7.75 GHz, uplink 7.9-8.4 GHz. Operated via WGS (Wideband Global SATCOM) constellation. Provides wideband data, video, and voice to tactical and strategic forces. WGS satellites provide up to 11 Gbps throughput. Protected from OBBBA reallocation (7.4-8.4 GHz excluded).
source: NTIA frequency allocation table; DTIC military satellite references

shared_with: Commercial maritime radar (9.3-9.5 GHz), weather radar
description: X-band military radar. Used for fire control, missile tracking, battlefield surveillance, and precision targeting. Includes AN/SPY-1 (Aegis), AN/TPY-2 (THAAD), various airborne radars. Also FAA TDWR (Terminal Doppler Weather Radar) at 5.6-5.65 GHz nearby. 9.0-9.2 GHz and 9.3-9.5 GHz shared with commercial maritime radar and radiolocation. Government maintains primary allocation throughout.
source: NTIA Federal Radar Spectrum Requirements (NTIA 00-40); NTIA spectrum allocation table

shared_with: Government primary allocation
description: Advanced Extremely High Frequency (AEHF) satellite downlink band (~20 GHz SHF). Provides protected, anti-jam, low probability of intercept/detect (LPI/LPD) communications for strategic nuclear command and control, senior leadership, and tactical forces. Successor to Milstar. AEHF satellites use frequency hopping and spread spectrum for survivability.
source: NTIA frequency allocation table; Wikipedia AEHF specifications

shared_with: Government primary allocation
description: Ka-band government satellite uplinks. Used for military SATCOM, intelligence community communications, and government earth stations. Part of the broader Ka-band (26.5-40 GHz) which is increasingly important for high-throughput satellite communications. Adjacent to commercial Ka-band allocations.
source: NTIA frequency allocation table

shared_with: Government exclusive for MILSATCOM
description: AEHF satellite uplink band (~44 GHz EHF). Extremely High Frequency provides maximum resistance to jamming and interception. Used for National Command Authority (NCA) nuclear command and control. AEHF constellation of 6 satellites provides global coverage. Narrowbeam antennas and frequency agility for survivability.
source: NTIA frequency allocation table; DoD AEHF program documentation
